title: Pain au chocolat
subtitle: Chocolate-batoned laminated French pastries
excerpt: Pain au chocolat made from the same laminated croissant dough, cut into rectangles around two thin chocolate batons and rolled. Twelve pastries from a two-day schedule.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Project Gutenberg (French): documents the laminated yeasted dough on which pain au chocolat is built. The pain au chocolat shape (rectangle with two chocolate batons) was established in early-twentieth-century Parisian boulangeries as a variant of the croissant.

recipe.freezeNotes: Shaped, unproofed pastries freeze well; defrost overnight in the fridge and proof from cold.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Two-day schedule like croissants: dough and lamination day 1, shape and bake day 2.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — drops to 180°C after 8 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Long thin sticks of dark chocolate (around 8 cm long, 8 mm thick) sold specifically for laminated pastries — they hold their shape in the bake without leaking through the dough. If unavailable, snap a chocolate bar into thin batons of similar dimensions, using a chocolate of at least 55% cocoa so it does not run.

content.text: Pain au chocolat uses the same laminated yeasted dough as croissants, cut and shaped differently: a rectangle of rolled dough wrapping two chocolate batons. The lamination work and the schedule are identical; only the cutting and shaping change. Treat this recipe as a continuation of the croissant method.

Dark chocolate batons are sold in baking-supply shops specifically for this use; they hold their shape through the bake. A standard chocolate bar broken into similarly-sized batons works in a pinch — use at least 55% cocoa so the chocolate stays firm rather than running through the dough.

items.prepNote: in batons; or broken from a bar at 55%+ cocoa

content.text: Mix the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{yeast-fast-action}} in a stand mixer. Add the cold {{whole-milk}} and softened first {{unsalted-butter}}. Mix on low for 6 minutes to a smooth dough. Shape into a 20×15 cm rectangle, wrap, chill 1 hour. Beat the second {{unsalted-butter}} between baking paper into a 20×15 cm rectangle and chill 30 minutes. Roll the dough to 40×20 cm, place the butter on one half and fold the other half over. Roll out to 60×20 cm and make a single fold (letter fold). Wrap and rest 30 minutes in the fridge. Repeat the roll-and-fold twice more, resting 30 minutes between each. Chill overnight after the third fold.

Roll the rested dough to a 45×30 cm rectangle, 4 mm thick. Trim square. Cut into 12 rectangles, each 11×10 cm. Place a baton of {{dark-chocolate}} along one short edge of each rectangle. Roll the dough over the baton once. Place a second baton against the first roll. Continue rolling tightly to the other end. Set seam-down on lined trays with 5 cm between each.

Proof at warm room temperature for about 2 hours until puffed and the layers are visible from the side. Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Brush with the beaten {{eggs}}. Bake at 200°C for 8 minutes, drop to 180°C, bake another 10 minutes; the pastries should be deep gold all over and the chocolate should be visible at the cut ends but not running out.
